metadata.description: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is an open standard file format and data interchange format that uses human-readable text to store and transmit data objects consisting of attribute–value pairs and arrays (or other serializable values).
metadata.title: How to load JSON | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

documents.page_content:
How to load JSON | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

[Skip to main content](#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)[](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/how_to/document_loader_json.mdx)# How to load JSON

[JSON (JavaScript Object Notation)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JSON) is an open standard file format and data interchange format that uses human-readable text to store and transmit data objects consisting of attribute–value pairs and arrays (or other serializable values).

[JSON Lines](https://jsonlines.org/) is a file format where each line is a valid JSON value.

LangChain implements a [JSONLoader](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/community/document_loaders/langchain_community.document_loaders.json_loader.JSONLoader.html)
to convert JSON and JSONL data into LangChain [Document](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/documents/langchain_core.documents.base.Document.html#langchain_core.documents.base.Document)
objects. It uses a specified [jq schema](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jq_(programming_language)) to parse the JSON files, allowing for the extraction of specific fields into the content
and metadata of the LangChain Document.

It uses the `jq` python package. Check out this [manual](https://stedolan.github.io/jq/manual/#Basicfilters) for a detailed documentation of the `jq` syntax.

Here we will demonstrate:

- How to load JSON and JSONL data into the content of a LangChain `Document`;

- How to load JSON and JSONL data into metadata associated with a `Document`.

```python
#!pip install jq
```

```python
from langchain_community.document_loaders import JSONLoader
```

**API Reference:**[JSONLoader](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/community/document_loaders/langchain_community.document_loaders.json_loader.JSONLoader.html)
```python
import json
from pathlib import Path
from pprint import pprint

file_path='./example_data/facebook_chat.json'
data = json.loads(Path(file_path).read_text())
```

```python
pprint(data)
```

metadata.description: This guide assumes familiarity with the following concepts:
metadata.title: How to stream runnables | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

metadata.description: This guide assumes familiarity with the following concepts:
metadata.title: How to parse JSON output | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

documents.page_content:
How to parse JSON output | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

[Skip to main content](#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)[](https://colab.research.google.com/github/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/how_to/output_parser_json.ipynb)[](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/how_to/output_parser_json.ipynb)# How to parse JSON output

PrerequisitesThis guide assumes familiarity with the following concepts:

- [Chat models](/docs/concepts/chat_models/)

- [Output parsers](/docs/concepts/output_parsers/)

- [Prompt templates](/docs/concepts/prompt_templates/)

- [Structured output](/docs/how_to/structured_output/)

- [Chaining runnables together](/docs/how_to/sequence/)

While some model providers support [built-in ways to return structured output](/docs/how_to/structured_output/), not all do. We can use an output parser to help users to specify an arbitrary JSON schema via the prompt, query a model for outputs that conform to that schema, and finally parse that schema as JSON.

noteKeep in mind that large language models are leaky abstractions! You'll have to use an LLM with sufficient capacity to generate well-formed JSON.

The [JsonOutputParser](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/output_parsers/langchain_core.output_parsers.json.JsonOutputParser.html) is one built-in option for prompting for and then parsing JSON output. While it is similar in functionality to the [PydanticOutputParser](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/output_parsers/langchain_core.output_parsers.pydantic.PydanticOutputParser.html), it also supports streaming back partial JSON objects.

Here's an example of how it can be used alongside [Pydantic](https://docs.pydantic.dev/) to conveniently declare the expected schema:

```python
%pip install -qU langchain langchain-openai

import os
from getpass import getpass

if "OPENAI_API_KEY" not in os.environ:
    os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"] = getpass()
```

```python
from langchain_core.output_parsers import JsonOutputParser
from langchain_core.prompts import PromptTemplate
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

model = ChatOpenAI(temperature=0)

# Define your desired data structure.
class Joke(BaseModel):
    setup: str = Field(description="question to set up a joke")
    punchline: str = Field(description="answer to resolve the joke")

# And a query intented to prompt a language model to populate the data structure.
joke_query = "Tell me a joke."

# Set up a parser + inject instructions into the prompt template.
parser = JsonOutputParser(pydantic_object=Joke)

prompt = PromptTemplate(
    template="Answer the user query.\n{format_instructions}\n{query}\n",
    input_variables=["query"],
    partial_variables={"format_instructions": parser.get_format_instructions()},
)

chain = prompt | model | parser

chain.invoke({"query": joke_query})
```

**API Reference:**[JsonOutputParser](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/output_parsers/langchain_core.output_parsers.json.JsonOutputParser.html) | [PromptTemplate](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/prompts/langchain_core.prompts.prompt.PromptTemplate.html) | [ChatOpenAI](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/openai/chat_models/langchain_openai.chat_models.base.ChatOpenAI.html)
```output
{'setup': "Why couldn't the bicycle stand up by itself?",
 'punchline': 'Because it was two tired!'}
```

Note that we are passing `format_instructions` from the parser directly into the prompt. You can and should experiment with adding your own formatting hints in the other parts of your prompt to either augment or replace the default instructions:

```python
parser.get_format_instructions()
```

documents.page_content:
How to load JSON | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

[Skip to main content](#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)[](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/how_to/document_loader_json.mdx)# How to load JSON

[JSON (JavaScript Object Notation)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JSON) is an open standard file format and data interchange format that uses human-readable text to store and transmit data objects consisting of attribute–value pairs and arrays (or other serializable values).

[JSON Lines](https://jsonlines.org/) is a file format where each line is a valid JSON value.

LangChain implements a [JSONLoader](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/community/document_loaders/langchain_community.document_loaders.json_loader.JSONLoader.html)
to convert JSON and JSONL data into LangChain [Document](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/documents/langchain_core.documents.base.Document.html#langchain_core.documents.base.Document)
objects. It uses a specified [jq schema](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jq_(programming_language)) to parse the JSON files, allowing for the extraction of specific fields into the content
and metadata of the LangChain Document.

It uses the `jq` python package. Check out this [manual](https://stedolan.github.io/jq/manual/#Basicfilters) for a detailed documentation of the `jq` syntax.

Here we will demonstrate:

- How to load JSON and JSONL data into the content of a LangChain `Document`;

- How to load JSON and JSONL data into metadata associated with a `Document`.

```python
#!pip install jq
```

```python
from langchain_community.document_loaders import JSONLoader
```

**API Reference:**[JSONLoader](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/community/document_loaders/langchain_community.document_loaders.json_loader.JSONLoader.html)
```python
import json
from pathlib import Path
from pprint import pprint

file_path='./example_data/facebook_chat.json'
data = json.loads(Path(file_path).read_text())
```

```python
pprint(data)
```

documents.page_content:
How to parse JSON output | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

[Skip to main content](#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)[](https://colab.research.google.com/github/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/how_to/output_parser_json.ipynb)[](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/how_to/output_parser_json.ipynb)# How to parse JSON output

PrerequisitesThis guide assumes familiarity with the following concepts:

- [Chat models](/docs/concepts/chat_models/)

- [Output parsers](/docs/concepts/output_parsers/)

- [Prompt templates](/docs/concepts/prompt_templates/)

- [Structured output](/docs/how_to/structured_output/)

- [Chaining runnables together](/docs/how_to/sequence/)

While some model providers support [built-in ways to return structured output](/docs/how_to/structured_output/), not all do. We can use an output parser to help users to specify an arbitrary JSON schema via the prompt, query a model for outputs that conform to that schema, and finally parse that schema as JSON.

noteKeep in mind that large language models are leaky abstractions! You'll have to use an LLM with sufficient capacity to generate well-formed JSON.

The [JsonOutputParser](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/output_parsers/langchain_core.output_parsers.json.JsonOutputParser.html) is one built-in option for prompting for and then parsing JSON output. While it is similar in functionality to the [PydanticOutputParser](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/output_parsers/langchain_core.output_parsers.pydantic.PydanticOutputParser.html), it also supports streaming back partial JSON objects.

Here's an example of how it can be used alongside [Pydantic](https://docs.pydantic.dev/) to conveniently declare the expected schema:

```python
%pip install -qU langchain langchain-openai

import os
from getpass import getpass

if "OPENAI_API_KEY" not in os.environ:
    os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"] = getpass()
```

```python
from langchain_core.output_parsers import JsonOutputParser
from langchain_core.prompts import PromptTemplate
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

model = ChatOpenAI(temperature=0)

# Define your desired data structure.
class Joke(BaseModel):
    setup: str = Field(description="question to set up a joke")
    punchline: str = Field(description="answer to resolve the joke")

# And a query intented to prompt a language model to populate the data structure.
joke_query = "Tell me a joke."

# Set up a parser + inject instructions into the prompt template.
parser = JsonOutputParser(pydantic_object=Joke)

prompt = PromptTemplate(
    template="Answer the user query.\n{format_instructions}\n{query}\n",
    input_variables=["query"],
    partial_variables={"format_instructions": parser.get_format_instructions()},
)

chain = prompt | model | parser

chain.invoke({"query": joke_query})
```

**API Reference:**[JsonOutputParser](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/output_parsers/langchain_core.output_parsers.json.JsonOutputParser.html) | [PromptTemplate](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/prompts/langchain_core.prompts.prompt.PromptTemplate.html) | [ChatOpenAI](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/openai/chat_models/langchain_openai.chat_models.base.ChatOpenAI.html)
```output
{'setup': "Why couldn't the bicycle stand up by itself?",
 'punchline': 'Because it was two tired!'}
```

Note that we are passing `format_instructions` from the parser directly into the prompt. You can and should experiment with adding your own formatting hints in the other parts of your prompt to either augment or replace the default instructions:

```python
parser.get_format_instructions()
```

```python
%pip install -qU langchain langchain-openai

import os

if "OPENAI_API_KEY" not in os.environ:
    os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"] = getpass()
```

```python
from langchain_core.output_parsers import JsonOutputParser
from langchain_core.prompts import PromptTemplate
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

model = ChatOpenAI(temperature=0)

class Joke(BaseModel):
    setup: str = Field(description="question to set up a joke")

metadata.description: Language processing systems generate text. However, there are situations where obtaining more organized data than plain text is desired. Although some service providers offer integrated methods for structured results, this is not universally available.
metadata.title: How to use output parsers to parse an LLM response into structured format | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

documents.page_content:
Output parsers accept a string or `BaseMessage` as input and can return an arbitrary type.

```python
parser.invoke(output)
```

```output
Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad dressing!')
```

Instead of manually invoking the parser, we also could've just added it to our `Runnable` sequence:

```python
chain = prompt | model | parser
chain.invoke({"query": "Tell me a joke."})
```

```output
Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad dressing!')
```

While all parsers support the streaming interface, only certain parsers can stream through partially parsed objects, since this is highly dependent on the output type. Parsers which cannot construct partial objects will simply yield the fully parsed output.

The `SimpleJsonOutputParser` for example can stream through partial outputs:

```python
from langchain.output_parsers.json import SimpleJsonOutputParser

json_prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template(
    "Return a JSON object with an `answer` key that answers the following question: {question}"
)
json_parser = SimpleJsonOutputParser()
json_chain = json_prompt | model | json_parser
```

**API Reference:**[SimpleJsonOutputParser](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/output_parsers/langchain_core.output_parsers.json.SimpleJsonOutputParser.html)
```python
list(json_chain.stream({"question": "Who invented the microscope?"}))
```

```output
[{},
 {'answer': ''},
 {'answer': 'Ant'},
 {'answer': 'Anton'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Lee'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Leeu'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Leeuwen'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Leeuwenho'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Leeuwenhoek'}]
```

Similarly,for `PydanticOutputParser`:

```python
list(chain.stream({"query": "Tell me a joke."}))
```

```output
[Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline=''),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad dressing'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad dressing!')]
```

#### Was this page helpful?

- [Get started](#get-started)

- [LCEL](#lcel)

metadata.description: This content discusses tools that process textual outputs from a system to convert them into a more organized and structured format.
metadata.title: Output parsers | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

documents.page_content:
Output parsers | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

[Skip to main content](#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)[](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/concepts/output_parsers.mdx)# Output parsers

noteThe information here refers to parsers that take a text output from a model try to parse it into a more structured representation.
More and more models are supporting function (or tool) calling, which handles this automatically.
It is recommended to use function/tool calling rather than output parsing.
See documentation for that [here](/docs/concepts/tool_calling/).

`Output parser` is responsible for taking the output of a model and transforming it to a more suitable format for downstream tasks.
Useful when you are using LLMs to generate structured data, or to normalize output from chat models and LLMs.

LangChain has lots of different types of output parsers. This is a list of output parsers LangChain supports. The table below has various pieces of information:

- **Name**: The name of the output parser

- **Supports Streaming**: Whether the output parser supports streaming.

- **Has Format Instructions**: Whether the output parser has format instructions. This is generally available except when (a) the desired schema is not specified in the prompt but rather in other parameters (like OpenAI function calling), or (b) when the OutputParser wraps another OutputParser.

- **Calls LLM**: Whether this output parser itself calls an LLM. This is usually only done by output parsers that attempt to correct misformatted output.

- **Input Type**: Expected input type. Most output parsers work on both strings and messages, but some (like OpenAI Functions) need a message with specific kwargs.

- **Output Type**: The output type of the object returned by the parser.

- **Description**: Our commentary on this output parser and when to use it.

metadata.description: A processing module was unable to interpret the system response correctly.

Note that some prebuilt constructs like [legacy LangChain agents](/docs/how_to/agent_executor) and chains may use output parsers internally,

## Troubleshooting

The following may help resolve this error:

metadata.description: Language processing systems generate text. However, there are situations where obtaining more organized data than plain text is preferred. Although some service providers offer integrated methods for structured results, this is not universally available.
metadata.title: How to use output parsers to parse an LLM response into structured format | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

documents.page_content:
Output parsers accept a string or `BaseMessage` as input and can return an arbitrary type.

```python
parser.invoke(output)
```

```output
Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad dressing!')
```

Instead of manually invoking the parser, we also could've just added it to our `Runnable` sequence:

```python
chain = prompt | model | parser
chain.invoke({"query": "Tell me a joke."})
```

```output
Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad dressing!')
```

While all parsers support the streaming interface, only certain parsers can stream through partially parsed objects, since this is highly dependent on the output type. Parsers which cannot construct partial objects will simply yield the fully parsed output.

The `SimpleJsonOutputParser` for example can stream through partial outputs:

```python
from langchain.output_parsers.json import SimpleJsonOutputParser

json_prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template(
    "Return a JSON object with an `answer` key that answers the following question: {question}"
)
json_parser = SimpleJsonOutputParser()
json_chain = json_prompt | model | json_parser
```

**API Reference:**[SimpleJsonOutputParser](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/output_parsers/langchain_core.output_parsers.json.SimpleJsonOutputParser.html)
```python
list(json_chain.stream({"question": "Who invented the microscope?"}))
```

```output
[{},
 {'answer': ''},
 {'answer': 'Ant'},
 {'answer': 'Anton'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Lee'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Leeu'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Leeuwen'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Leeuwenho'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Leeuwenhoek'}]
```

Similarly,for `PydanticOutputParser`:

```python
list(chain.stream({"query": "Tell me a joke."}))
```

```output
[Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline=''),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad dressing'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad dressing!')]
```

#### Was this page helpful?

- [Get started](#get-started)

- [LCEL](#lcel)

documents.page_content:
How to parse JSON output | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

[Skip to main content](#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)[](https://colab.research.google.com/github/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/how_to/output_parser_json.ipynb)[](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/how_to/output_parser_json.ipynb)# How to parse JSON output

PrerequisitesThis guide assumes familiarity with the following concepts:

- [Chat models](/docs/concepts/chat_models/)

- [Output parsers](/docs/concepts/output_parsers/)

- [Prompt templates](/docs/concepts/prompt_templates/)

- [Structured output](/docs/how_to/structured_output/)

- [Chaining runnables together](/docs/how_to/sequence/)

While some model providers support [built-in ways to return structured output](/docs/how_to/structured_output/), not all do. We can use an output parser to help users to specify an arbitrary JSON schema via the prompt, query a model for outputs that conform to that schema, and finally parse that schema as JSON.

noteKeep in mind that large language models are leaky abstractions! You'll have to use an LLM with sufficient capacity to generate well-formed JSON.

The [JsonOutputParser](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/output_parsers/langchain_core.output_parsers.json.JsonOutputParser.html) is one built-in option for prompting for and then parsing JSON output. While it is similar in functionality to the [PydanticOutputParser](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/output_parsers/langchain_core.output_parsers.pydantic.PydanticOutputParser.html), it also supports streaming back partial JSON objects.

Here's an example of how it can be used alongside [Pydantic](https://docs.pydantic.dev/) to conveniently declare the expected schema:

```python
%pip install -qU langchain langchain-openai

import os
from getpass import getpass

if "OPENAI_API_KEY" not in os.environ:
    os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"] = getpass()
```

```python
from langchain_core.output_parsers import JsonOutputParser
from langchain_core.prompts import PromptTemplate
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

model = ChatOpenAI(temperature=0)

# Define your desired data structure.
class Joke(BaseModel):
    setup: str = Field(description="question to set up a joke")
    punchline: str = Field(description="answer to resolve the joke")

# And a query intented to prompt a language model to populate the data structure.
joke_query = "Tell me a joke."

# Set up a parser + inject instructions into the prompt template.
parser = JsonOutputParser(pydantic_object=Joke)

prompt = PromptTemplate(
    template="Answer the user query.\n{format_instructions}\n{query}\n",
    input_variables=["query"],
    partial_variables={"format_instructions": parser.get_format_instructions()},
)

chain = prompt | model | parser

chain.invoke({"query": joke_query})
```

**API Reference:**[JsonOutputParser](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/output_parsers/langchain_core.output_parsers.json.JsonOutputParser.html) | [PromptTemplate](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/prompts/langchain_core.prompts.prompt.PromptTemplate.html) | [ChatOpenAI](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/openai/chat_models/langchain_openai.chat_models.base.ChatOpenAI.html)
```output
{'setup': "Why couldn't the bicycle stand up by itself?",
 'punchline': 'Because it was two tired!'}
```

Note that we are passing `format_instructions` from the parser directly into the prompt. You can and should experiment with adding your own formatting hints in the other parts of your prompt to either augment or replace the default instructions:

```python
parser.get_format_instructions()
```

metadata.description: This section discusses tools that process textual outputs from a system to convert them into a more organized and structured format.
metadata.title: Output parsers | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

documents.page_content:
Output parsers | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

[Skip to main content](#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)[](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/concepts/output_parsers.mdx)# Output parsers

noteThe information here refers to parsers that take a text output from a model try to parse it into a more structured representation.
More and more models are supporting function (or tool) calling, which handles this automatically.
It is recommended to use function/tool calling rather than output parsing.
See documentation for that [here](/docs/concepts/tool_calling/).

`Output parser` is responsible for taking the output of a model and transforming it to a more suitable format for downstream tasks.
Useful when you are using LLMs to generate structured data, or to normalize output from chat models and LLMs.

LangChain has lots of different types of output parsers. This is a list of output parsers LangChain supports. The table below has various pieces of information:

- **Name**: The name of the output parser

- **Supports Streaming**: Whether the output parser supports streaming.

- **Has Format Instructions**: Whether the output parser has format instructions. This is generally available except when (a) the desired schema is not specified in the prompt but rather in other parameters (like OpenAI function calling), or (b) when the OutputParser wraps another OutputParser.

- **Calls LLM**: Whether this output parser itself calls an LLM. This is usually only done by output parsers that attempt to correct misformatted output.

- **Input Type**: Expected input type. Most output parsers work on both strings and messages, but some (like OpenAI Functions) need a message with specific kwargs.

- **Output Type**: The output type of the object returned by the parser.

- **Description**: Our commentary on this output parser and when to use it.

    192     if isinstance(input, BaseMessage):

--> 194             lambda inner_input: self.parse_result(

``````output

     84 except JSONDecodeError as e:
     85     msg = f"Invalid json output: {text}"
---> 86     raise OutputParserException(msg, llm_output=text) from e
``````output
OutputParserException: Invalid json output: ```
{{"foo":
```

```text

Note that some prebuilt constructs like [legacy LangChain agents](/docs/how_to/agent_executor) and chains may use output parsers internally,
so you may see this error even if you're not visibly instantiating and using an output parser.

documents.page_content:
How to parse JSON output | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

[Skip to main content](#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)[](https://colab.research.google.com/github/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/how_to/output_parser_json.ipynb)[](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/how_to/output_parser_json.ipynb)# How to parse JSON output

PrerequisitesThis guide assumes familiarity with the following concepts:

- [Chat models](/docs/concepts/chat_models/)

- [Output parsers](/docs/concepts/output_parsers/)

- [Prompt templates](/docs/concepts/prompt_templates/)

- [Structured output](/docs/how_to/structured_output/)

- [Chaining runnables together](/docs/how_to/sequence/)

While some model providers support [built-in ways to return structured output](/docs/how_to/structured_output/), not all do. We can use an output parser to help users to specify an arbitrary JSON schema via the prompt, query a model for outputs that conform to that schema, and finally parse that schema as JSON.

noteKeep in mind that large language models are leaky abstractions! You'll have to use an LLM with sufficient capacity to generate well-formed JSON.

The [JsonOutputParser](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/output_parsers/langchain_core.output_parsers.json.JsonOutputParser.html) is one built-in option for prompting for and then parsing JSON output. While it is similar in functionality to the [PydanticOutputParser](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/output_parsers/langchain_core.output_parsers.pydantic.PydanticOutputParser.html), it also supports streaming back partial JSON objects.

Here's an example of how it can be used alongside [Pydantic](https://docs.pydantic.dev/) to conveniently declare the expected schema:

```python
%pip install -qU langchain langchain-openai

import os
from getpass import getpass

if "OPENAI_API_KEY" not in os.environ:
    os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"] = getpass()
```

```python
from langchain_core.output_parsers import JsonOutputParser
from langchain_core.prompts import PromptTemplate
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

model = ChatOpenAI(temperature=0)

# Define your desired data structure.
class Joke(BaseModel):
    setup: str = Field(description="question to set up a joke")
    punchline: str = Field(description="answer to resolve the joke")

# And a query intented to prompt a language model to populate the data structure.
joke_query = "Tell me a joke."

# Set up a parser + inject instructions into the prompt template.
parser = JsonOutputParser(pydantic_object=Joke)

prompt = PromptTemplate(
    template="Answer the user query.\n{format_instructions}\n{query}\n",
    input_variables=["query"],
    partial_variables={"format_instructions": parser.get_format_instructions()},
)

chain = prompt | model | parser

chain.invoke({"query": joke_query})
```

**API Reference:**[JsonOutputParser](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/output_parsers/langchain_core.output_parsers.json.JsonOutputParser.html) | [PromptTemplate](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/prompts/langchain_core.prompts.prompt.PromptTemplate.html) | [ChatOpenAI](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/openai/chat_models/langchain_openai.chat_models.base.ChatOpenAI.html)
```output
{'setup': "Why couldn't the bicycle stand up by itself?",
 'punchline': 'Because it was two tired!'}
```

Note that we are passing `format_instructions` from the parser directly into the prompt. You can and should experiment with adding your own formatting hints in the other parts of your prompt to either augment or replace the default instructions:

```python
parser.get_format_instructions()
```

documents.page_content:
Output parsers | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

[Skip to main content](#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)[](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/concepts/output_parsers.mdx)# Output parsers

noteThe information here refers to parsers that take a text output from a model try to parse it into a more structured representation.
More and more models are supporting function (or tool) calling, which handles this automatically.
It is recommended to use function/tool calling rather than output parsing.
See documentation for that [here](/docs/concepts/tool_calling/).

`Output parser` is responsible for taking the output of a model and transforming it to a more suitable format for downstream tasks.
Useful when you are using LLMs to generate structured data, or to normalize output from chat models and LLMs.

LangChain has lots of different types of output parsers. This is a list of output parsers LangChain supports. The table below has various pieces of information:

- **Name**: The name of the output parser

- **Supports Streaming**: Whether the output parser supports streaming.

- **Has Format Instructions**: Whether the output parser has format instructions. This is generally available except when (a) the desired schema is not specified in the prompt but rather in other parameters (like OpenAI function calling), or (b) when the OutputParser wraps another OutputParser.

- **Calls LLM**: Whether this output parser itself calls an LLM. This is usually only done by output parsers that attempt to correct misformatted output.

- **Input Type**: Expected input type. Most output parsers work on both strings and messages, but some (like OpenAI Functions) need a message with specific kwargs.

- **Output Type**: The output type of the object returned by the parser.

- **Description**: Our commentary on this output parser and when to use it.

     84 except JSONDecodeError as e:

---> 86     raise OutputParserException(msg, llm_output=text) from e

```

```text

Note that some prebuilt constructs like [legacy LangChain agents](/docs/how_to/agent_executor) and chains may use output parsers internally,
so you may see this error even if you're not visibly instantiating and using an output parser.

## Troubleshooting

The following may help resolve this error:

Output parsers | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

[Skip to main content](#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)[](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/concepts/output_parsers.mdx)# Output parsers

noteThe information here refers to parsers that take a text output from a model try to parse it into a more structured representation.

See documentation for that [here](/docs/concepts/tool_calling/).

metadata.description: AI systems generate text responses. However, there are situations where obtaining more organized data is preferred over plain text. Although some service providers offer integrated methods for structured results, this feature is not universally available.
metadata.title: How to use output parsers to parse an LLM response into structured format | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

documents.page_content:
Output parsers accept a string or `BaseMessage` as input and can return an arbitrary type.

```python
parser.invoke(output)
```

```output
Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad dressing!')
```

Instead of manually invoking the parser, we also could've just added it to our `Runnable` sequence:

```python
chain = prompt | model | parser
chain.invoke({"query": "Tell me a joke."})
```

```output
Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad dressing!')
```

While all parsers support the streaming interface, only certain parsers can stream through partially parsed objects, since this is highly dependent on the output type. Parsers which cannot construct partial objects will simply yield the fully parsed output.

The `SimpleJsonOutputParser` for example can stream through partial outputs:

```python
from langchain.output_parsers.json import SimpleJsonOutputParser

json_prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template(
    "Return a JSON object with an `answer` key that answers the following question: {question}"
)
json_parser = SimpleJsonOutputParser()
json_chain = json_prompt | model | json_parser
```

**API Reference:**[SimpleJsonOutputParser](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/output_parsers/langchain_core.output_parsers.json.SimpleJsonOutputParser.html)
```python
list(json_chain.stream({"question": "Who invented the microscope?"}))
```

```output
[{},
 {'answer': ''},
 {'answer': 'Ant'},
 {'answer': 'Anton'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Lee'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Leeu'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Leeuwen'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Leeuwenho'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Leeuwenhoek'}]
```

Similarly,for `PydanticOutputParser`:

```python
list(chain.stream({"query": "Tell me a joke."}))
```

```output
[Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline=''),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad dressing'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad dressing!')]
```

#### Was this page helpful?

- [Get started](#get-started)

- [LCEL](#lcel)

{'setup': "Why couldn't the bicycle stand up by itself?",
 'punchline': 'Because it was two tired!'}
```

Note that we are passing `format_instructions` from the parser directly into the prompt. You can and should experiment with adding your own formatting hints in the other parts of your prompt to either augment or replace the default instructions:

```python
parser.get_format_instructions()
```

documents.page_content:
Output parsers | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

[Skip to main content](#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)[](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/concepts/output_parsers.mdx)# Output parsers

noteThe information here refers to parsers that take a text output from a model try to parse it into a more structured representation.
More and more models are supporting function (or tool) calling, which handles this automatically.
It is recommended to use function/tool calling rather than output parsing.
See documentation for that [here](/docs/concepts/tool_calling/).

`Output parser` is responsible for taking the output of a model and transforming it to a more suitable format for downstream tasks.
Useful when you are using LLMs to generate structured data, or to normalize output from chat models and LLMs.

LangChain has lots of different types of output parsers. This is a list of output parsers LangChain supports. The table below has various pieces of information:

- **Name**: The name of the output parser

- **Supports Streaming**: Whether the output parser supports streaming.

- **Has Format Instructions**: Whether the output parser has format instructions. This is generally available except when (a) the desired schema is not specified in the prompt but rather in other parameters (like OpenAI function calling), or (b) when the OutputParser wraps another OutputParser.

- **Calls LLM**: Whether this output parser itself calls an LLM. This is usually only done by output parsers that attempt to correct misformatted output.

- **Input Type**: Expected input type. Most output parsers work on both strings and messages, but some (like OpenAI Functions) need a message with specific kwargs.

- **Output Type**: The output type of the object returned by the parser.

- **Description**: Our commentary on this output parser and when to use it.

metadata.description: Language processing systems generate text. However, there are situations where obtaining more organized data than plain text is preferred. Although some service providers offer integrated methods for structured results, this is not universally available.
metadata.title: How to use output parsers to parse an LLM response into structured format | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

documents.page_content:
Output parsers accept a string or `BaseMessage` as input and can return an arbitrary type.

```python
parser.invoke(output)
```

```output
Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad dressing!')
```

Instead of manually invoking the parser, we also could've just added it to our `Runnable` sequence:

```python
chain = prompt | model | parser
chain.invoke({"query": "Tell me a joke."})
```

```output
Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad dressing!')
```

While all parsers support the streaming interface, only certain parsers can stream through partially parsed objects, since this is highly dependent on the output type. Parsers which cannot construct partial objects will simply yield the fully parsed output.

The `SimpleJsonOutputParser` for example can stream through partial outputs:

```python
from langchain.output_parsers.json import SimpleJsonOutputParser

json_prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template(
    "Return a JSON object with an `answer` key that answers the following question: {question}"
)
json_parser = SimpleJsonOutputParser()
json_chain = json_prompt | model | json_parser
```

**API Reference:**[SimpleJsonOutputParser](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/output_parsers/langchain_core.output_parsers.json.SimpleJsonOutputParser.html)
```python
list(json_chain.stream({"question": "Who invented the microscope?"}))
```

```output
[{},
 {'answer': ''},
 {'answer': 'Ant'},
 {'answer': 'Anton'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Lee'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Leeu'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Leeuwen'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Leeuwenho'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Leeuwenhoek'}]
```

Similarly,for `PydanticOutputParser`:

```python
list(chain.stream({"query": "Tell me a joke."}))
```

```output
[Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline=''),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad dressing'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad dressing!')]
```

#### Was this page helpful?

- [Get started](#get-started)

- [LCEL](#lcel)

metadata.title: How to parse JSON output | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

documents.page_content:
How to parse JSON output | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

[Skip to main content](#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)[](https://colab.research.google.com/github/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/how_to/output_parser_json.ipynb)[](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/how_to/output_parser_json.ipynb)# How to parse JSON output

PrerequisitesThis guide assumes familiarity with the following concepts:

- [Chat models](/docs/concepts/chat_models/)

- [Output parsers](/docs/concepts/output_parsers/)

- [Prompt templates](/docs/concepts/prompt_templates/)

- [Structured output](/docs/how_to/structured_output/)

- [Chaining runnables together](/docs/how_to/sequence/)

While some model providers support [built-in ways to return structured output](/docs/how_to/structured_output/), not all do. We can use an output parser to help users to specify an arbitrary JSON schema via the prompt, query a model for outputs that conform to that schema, and finally parse that schema as JSON.

noteKeep in mind that large language models are leaky abstractions! You'll have to use an LLM with sufficient capacity to generate well-formed JSON.

The [JsonOutputParser](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/output_parsers/langchain_core.output_parsers.json.JsonOutputParser.html) is one built-in option for prompting for and then parsing JSON output. While it is similar in functionality to the [PydanticOutputParser](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/output_parsers/langchain_core.output_parsers.pydantic.PydanticOutputParser.html), it also supports streaming back partial JSON objects.

Here's an example of how it can be used alongside [Pydantic](https://docs.pydantic.dev/) to conveniently declare the expected schema:

```python
%pip install -qU langchain langchain-openai

import os
from getpass import getpass

if "OPENAI_API_KEY" not in os.environ:
    os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"] = getpass()
```

```python
from langchain_core.output_parsers import JsonOutputParser
from langchain_core.prompts import PromptTemplate
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

model = ChatOpenAI(temperature=0)

# Define your desired data structure.
class Joke(BaseModel):
    setup: str = Field(description="question to set up a joke")
    punchline: str = Field(description="answer to resolve the joke")

# And a query intented to prompt a language model to populate the data structure.
joke_query = "Tell me a joke."

# Set up a parser + inject instructions into the prompt template.
parser = JsonOutputParser(pydantic_object=Joke)

prompt = PromptTemplate(
    template="Answer the user query.\n{format_instructions}\n{query}\n",
    input_variables=["query"],
    partial_variables={"format_instructions": parser.get_format_instructions()},
)

chain = prompt | model | parser

chain.invoke({"query": joke_query})
```

**API Reference:**[JsonOutputParser](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/output_parsers/langchain_core.output_parsers.json.JsonOutputParser.html) | [PromptTemplate](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/prompts/langchain_core.prompts.prompt.PromptTemplate.html) | [ChatOpenAI](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/openai/chat_models/langchain_openai.chat_models.base.ChatOpenAI.html)
```output
{'setup': "Why couldn't the bicycle stand up by itself?",
 'punchline': 'Because it was two tired!'}
```

Note that we are passing `format_instructions` from the parser directly into the prompt. You can and should experiment with adding your own formatting hints in the other parts of your prompt to either augment or replace the default instructions:

```python
parser.get_format_instructions()
```

metadata.description: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is an open standard file format and data interchange format that uses human-readable text to store and transmit data objects consisting of attribute–value pairs and arrays (or other serializable values).
metadata.title: How to load JSON | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

documents.page_content:
How to load JSON | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

[Skip to main content](#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)[](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/how_to/document_loader_json.mdx)# How to load JSON

[JSON (JavaScript Object Notation)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JSON) is an open standard file format and data interchange format that uses human-readable text to store and transmit data objects consisting of attribute–value pairs and arrays (or other serializable values).

[JSON Lines](https://jsonlines.org/) is a file format where each line is a valid JSON value.

LangChain implements a [JSONLoader](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/community/document_loaders/langchain_community.document_loaders.json_loader.JSONLoader.html)
to convert JSON and JSONL data into LangChain [Document](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/documents/langchain_core.documents.base.Document.html#langchain_core.documents.base.Document)
objects. It uses a specified [jq schema](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jq_(programming_language)) to parse the JSON files, allowing for the extraction of specific fields into the content
and metadata of the LangChain Document.

It uses the `jq` python package. Check out this [manual](https://stedolan.github.io/jq/manual/#Basicfilters) for a detailed documentation of the `jq` syntax.

Here we will demonstrate:

- How to load JSON and JSONL data into the content of a LangChain `Document`;

- How to load JSON and JSONL data into metadata associated with a `Document`.

```python
#!pip install jq
```

```python
from langchain_community.document_loaders import JSONLoader
```

**API Reference:**[JSONLoader](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/community/document_loaders/langchain_community.document_loaders.json_loader.JSONLoader.html)
```python
import json
from pathlib import Path
from pprint import pprint

file_path='./example_data/facebook_chat.json'
data = json.loads(Path(file_path).read_text())
```

```python
pprint(data)
```

```output
Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad dressing!')
```

While all parsers support the streaming interface, only certain parsers can stream through partially parsed objects, since this is highly dependent on the output type. Parsers which cannot construct partial objects will simply yield the fully parsed output.

The `SimpleJsonOutputParser` for example can stream through partial outputs:

```python
from langchain.output_parsers.json import SimpleJsonOutputParser

json_prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template(
    "Return a JSON object with an `answer` key that answers the following question: {question}"
)
json_parser = SimpleJsonOutputParser()
json_chain = json_prompt | model | json_parser
```

**API Reference:**[SimpleJsonOutputParser](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/output_parsers/langchain_core.output_parsers.json.SimpleJsonOutputParser.html)
```python
list(json_chain.stream({"question": "Who invented the microscope?"}))
```

```output
[{},
 {'answer': ''},
 {'answer': 'Ant'},
 {'answer': 'Anton'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Lee'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Leeu'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Leeuwen'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Leeuwenho'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Leeuwenhoek'}]
```

Similarly,for `PydanticOutputParser`:

```python
list(chain.stream({"query": "Tell me a joke."}))
```

```output
[Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline=''),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad dressing'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad dressing!')]
```

- [LCEL](#lcel)

documents.page_content:
Output parsers | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

[Skip to main content](#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)[](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/concepts/output_parsers.mdx)# Output parsers

noteThe information here refers to parsers that take a text output from a model try to parse it into a more structured representation.
More and more models are supporting function (or tool) calling, which handles this automatically.
It is recommended to use function/tool calling rather than output parsing.
See documentation for that [here](/docs/concepts/tool_calling/).

`Output parser` is responsible for taking the output of a model and transforming it to a more suitable format for downstream tasks.
Useful when you are using LLMs to generate structured data, or to normalize output from chat models and LLMs.

LangChain has lots of different types of output parsers. This is a list of output parsers LangChain supports. The table below has various pieces of information:

- **Name**: The name of the output parser

- **Supports Streaming**: Whether the output parser supports streaming.

- **Has Format Instructions**: Whether the output parser has format instructions. This is generally available except when (a) the desired schema is not specified in the prompt but rather in other parameters (like OpenAI function calling), or (b) when the OutputParser wraps another OutputParser.

- **Calls LLM**: Whether this output parser itself calls an LLM. This is usually only done by output parsers that attempt to correct misformatted output.

- **Input Type**: Expected input type. Most output parsers work on both strings and messages, but some (like OpenAI Functions) need a message with specific kwargs.

- **Output Type**: The output type of the object returned by the parser.

- **Description**: Our commentary on this output parser and when to use it.

metadata.description: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is an open standard file format and data interchange format that uses human-readable text to store and transmit data objects consisting of attribute–value pairs and arrays (or other serializable values).
metadata.title: How to load JSON | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

documents.page_content:
How to load JSON | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

[Skip to main content](#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)[](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/how_to/document_loader_json.mdx)# How to load JSON

[JSON (JavaScript Object Notation)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JSON) is an open standard file format and data interchange format that uses human-readable text to store and transmit data objects consisting of attribute–value pairs and arrays (or other serializable values).

[JSON Lines](https://jsonlines.org/) is a file format where each line is a valid JSON value.

LangChain implements a [JSONLoader](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/community/document_loaders/langchain_community.document_loaders.json_loader.JSONLoader.html)
to convert JSON and JSONL data into LangChain [Document](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/documents/langchain_core.documents.base.Document.html#langchain_core.documents.base.Document)
objects. It uses a specified [jq schema](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jq_(programming_language)) to parse the JSON files, allowing for the extraction of specific fields into the content
and metadata of the LangChain Document.

It uses the `jq` python package. Check out this [manual](https://stedolan.github.io/jq/manual/#Basicfilters) for a detailed documentation of the `jq` syntax.

Here we will demonstrate:

- How to load JSON and JSONL data into the content of a LangChain `Document`;

- How to load JSON and JSONL data into metadata associated with a `Document`.

```python
#!pip install jq
```

```python
from langchain_community.document_loaders import JSONLoader
```

**API Reference:**[JSONLoader](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/community/document_loaders/langchain_community.document_loaders.json_loader.JSONLoader.html)
```python
import json
from pathlib import Path
from pprint import pprint

file_path='./example_data/facebook_chat.json'
data = json.loads(Path(file_path).read_text())
```

```python
pprint(data)
```

metadata.title: How to parse JSON output | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

documents.page_content:
How to parse JSON output | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

[Skip to main content](#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)[](https://colab.research.google.com/github/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/how_to/output_parser_json.ipynb)[](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/how_to/output_parser_json.ipynb)# How to parse JSON output

PrerequisitesThis guide assumes familiarity with the following concepts:

- [Chat models](/docs/concepts/chat_models/)

- [Output parsers](/docs/concepts/output_parsers/)

- [Prompt templates](/docs/concepts/prompt_templates/)

- [Structured output](/docs/how_to/structured_output/)

- [Chaining runnables together](/docs/how_to/sequence/)

While some model providers support [built-in ways to return structured output](/docs/how_to/structured_output/), not all do. We can use an output parser to help users to specify an arbitrary JSON schema via the prompt, query a model for outputs that conform to that schema, and finally parse that schema as JSON.

noteKeep in mind that large language models are leaky abstractions! You'll have to use an LLM with sufficient capacity to generate well-formed JSON.

The [JsonOutputParser](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/output_parsers/langchain_core.output_parsers.json.JsonOutputParser.html) is one built-in option for prompting for and then parsing JSON output. While it is similar in functionality to the [PydanticOutputParser](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/output_parsers/langchain_core.output_parsers.pydantic.PydanticOutputParser.html), it also supports streaming back partial JSON objects.

Here's an example of how it can be used alongside [Pydantic](https://docs.pydantic.dev/) to conveniently declare the expected schema:

```python
%pip install -qU langchain langchain-openai

import os
from getpass import getpass

if "OPENAI_API_KEY" not in os.environ:
    os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"] = getpass()
```

```python
from langchain_core.output_parsers import JsonOutputParser
from langchain_core.prompts import PromptTemplate
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

model = ChatOpenAI(temperature=0)

# Define your desired data structure.
class Joke(BaseModel):
    setup: str = Field(description="question to set up a joke")
    punchline: str = Field(description="answer to resolve the joke")

# And a query intented to prompt a language model to populate the data structure.
joke_query = "Tell me a joke."

# Set up a parser + inject instructions into the prompt template.
parser = JsonOutputParser(pydantic_object=Joke)

prompt = PromptTemplate(
    template="Answer the user query.\n{format_instructions}\n{query}\n",
    input_variables=["query"],
    partial_variables={"format_instructions": parser.get_format_instructions()},
)

chain = prompt | model | parser

chain.invoke({"query": joke_query})
```

**API Reference:**[JsonOutputParser](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/output_parsers/langchain_core.output_parsers.json.JsonOutputParser.html) | [PromptTemplate](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/prompts/langchain_core.prompts.prompt.PromptTemplate.html) | [ChatOpenAI](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/openai/chat_models/langchain_openai.chat_models.base.ChatOpenAI.html)
```output
{'setup': "Why couldn't the bicycle stand up by itself?",
 'punchline': 'Because it was two tired!'}
```

Note that we are passing `format_instructions` from the parser directly into the prompt. You can and should experiment with adding your own formatting hints in the other parts of your prompt to either augment or replace the default instructions:

```python
parser.get_format_instructions()
```

metadata.description: Language processing systems generate text. However, there are situations where obtaining more organized data than plain text is preferred. Although some service providers offer integrated methods for structured results, this is not universally available.
metadata.title: How to use output parsers to parse an LLM response into structured format | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

documents.page_content:
Output parsers accept a string or `BaseMessage` as input and can return an arbitrary type.

```python
parser.invoke(output)
```

```output
Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad dressing!')
```

Instead of manually invoking the parser, we also could've just added it to our `Runnable` sequence:

```python
chain = prompt | model | parser
chain.invoke({"query": "Tell me a joke."})
```

```output
Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad dressing!')
```

While all parsers support the streaming interface, only certain parsers can stream through partially parsed objects, since this is highly dependent on the output type. Parsers which cannot construct partial objects will simply yield the fully parsed output.

The `SimpleJsonOutputParser` for example can stream through partial outputs:

```python
from langchain.output_parsers.json import SimpleJsonOutputParser

json_prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template(
    "Return a JSON object with an `answer` key that answers the following question: {question}"
)
json_parser = SimpleJsonOutputParser()
json_chain = json_prompt | model | json_parser
```

**API Reference:**[SimpleJsonOutputParser](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/output_parsers/langchain_core.output_parsers.json.SimpleJsonOutputParser.html)
```python
list(json_chain.stream({"question": "Who invented the microscope?"}))
```

```output
[{},
 {'answer': ''},
 {'answer': 'Ant'},
 {'answer': 'Anton'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Lee'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Leeu'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Leeuwen'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Leeuwenho'},
 {'answer': 'Antonie van Leeuwenhoek'}]
```

Similarly,for `PydanticOutputParser`:

```python
list(chain.stream({"query": "Tell me a joke."}))
```

```output
[Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline=''),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad dressing'),
 Joke(setup='Why did the tomato turn red?', punchline='Because it saw the salad dressing!')]
```

#### Was this page helpful?

- [LCEL](#lcel)

metadata.description: The evaluation tool offers pre-built evaluators suitable for standard assessment tasks.
metadata.title: LangChain off-the-shelf evaluators | 🦜️🛠️ LangSmith

documents.page_content: LangChain off-the-shelf evaluators | 🦜️🛠️ LangSmith

steps: Compare their implementations and use cases
Provide code examples and best practices
Recommend which parser to use in different scenarios

messages.content:
I have this code - prompt_template = PromptTemplate.from_template("Please provide a humorous remark about {subject}")

message  = prompt_template.invoke({"subject": "animals"})

chatModel.invoke(message) --- and I have this code ---> prompt_template = "Convert the following English sentence to another language: {sentence}"

prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template(prompt_template)

formatted_prompt = prompt.format(sentence="Good morning, I am a visitor.")
formatted_prompt --- Why do we use invoke twice in the above example, and how is it different from the following? Which method is generally preferred?

Certainly! Here's a fully anonymized and generalized version of your text, maintaining the original structure, length, and intent:

---

Let me clarify the differences between these two approaches and explain the usage of `invoke()`:

1. First Code Snippet:
```python
prompt_template = PromptTemplate.from_template("Generate a fun fact about {subject}")
message = prompt_template.invoke({"subject": "birds"})
languageModel.invoke(message)
```

2. Second Code Snippet:
```python
prompt_template = "Convert the following English sentence to Spanish: {sentence}"
prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template(prompt_template)
formatted_prompt = prompt.format(sentence="Good morning, I am a student.")
formatted_prompt
```

Key Differences:

1. Method of Formatting:
- The first snippet uses `invoke()`: This method both formats the template and produces a complete message object.
- The second snippet uses `format()`: This method simply replaces the placeholder with the provided value.

2. Return Type:
- `invoke()` returns a `PromptValue` object
- `format()` returns a string

3. Recommended Approach:
The first approach (using `invoke()`) is generally favored in frameworks similar to this because:
- It aligns better with chain-based workflows
- It produces a standardized `PromptValue` that can be directly used with language models
- It offers greater flexibility and is integrated into the main processing pipeline

Suggested Modern Method:
```python
# Recommended method
prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template("Generate a fun fact about {subject}")
chain = prompt | languageModel  # Using a chain expression syntax
result = chain.invoke({"subject": "birds"})
```

This method:
- Utilizes the pipe (`|`) operator
- Builds a complete processing chain
- Facilitates component composition
- Is the idiomatic approach in recent versions of the framework

Detailed Explanation of `invoke()`:
- `invoke()` does more than just string substitution
- It constructs a structured message that includes:
  - The formatted prompt
  - Metadata about the prompt
  - Compatibility with various model interfaces

Example to illustrate the difference:
```python
# Using format()
formatted_text = prompt.format(sentence="Good morning")
print(type(formatted_text))  # <class 'str'>

# Using invoke()
prompt_value = prompt.invoke({"sentence": "Good morning"})
print(type(prompt_value))  # <class 'PromptValue'>
```

When to Use Each:
- Use `invoke()` when working within chain-based workflows and with language models
- Use `format()` for simple string templating without model interaction
- Prefer the chain syntax (`prompt | model`) for modern workflows

Best Practices:
1. Use `invoke()` when integrating with language models
2. Use `format()` for straightforward string formatting
3. Use the pipe (`|`) operator for constructing chains

Let's analyze the issues with the original code and clarify why the revised approach is effective:

Potential Issues in the Original Code:
1. Direct Conversion from a General Model
```python
workflow = chat_template | languageModel
response = workflow.invoke({...})
```
The main concern here is that the general language model interface doesn't automatically handle the conversion of template parameters into a structured chat message format.

Detailed Explanation of the Corrected Approach:
```python
workflow = (
    RunnablePassthrough.assign(
        messages=lambda x: chat_template.format_messages(**x)
    ) 
    | (lambda x: x['messages'])  # Extract only the formatted messages
    | languageModel
    | StringOutputParser()
)
```

Step-by-step Breakdown:

1. `RunnablePassthrough.assign()`:
   - Creates a new dictionary that retains all original input data
   - Adds a new key `messages`
   - Uses `format_messages()` to transform input parameters into properly formatted chat messages
   - Ensures that the message structure is correct before passing to the language model

2. `lambda x: x['messages']`:
   - Extracts just the list of formatted chat messages
   - Guarantees that only the properly formatted messages are sent to the model

3. `| languageModel`:
   - Sends the chat messages to the language processing model

4. `| StringOutputParser()`:
   - Converts the model's output into a simple string format

Common Pitfalls to Avoid:
1. Assuming that direct conversion works universally for all templates
2. Confusing prompt templates with chat message formatting
3. Combining text completion models with chat-specific models without proper handling

Recommended Best Practices:
```python
# Always utilize ChatPromptTemplate for chat-based models
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate

# Define a chat-specific prompt template
chat_template = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are an {expertise} specialist."),
    ("human", "Discuss a {subject}"),
    ("human", "{user_input}")
])

# Build a resilient processing chain
workflow = (
    RunnablePassthrough.assign(
        messages=lambda x: chat_template.format_messages(**x)
    ) 
    | (lambda x: x['messages'])
    | languageModel
    | StringOutputParser()
)
```

Alternative Robust Methods:
1. Using `RunnableLambda`:
```python
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableLambda

def format_messages(input_dict):
    return chat_template.format_messages(**input_dict)

workflow = (
    RunnableLambda(format_messages)
    | languageModel
    | StringOutputParser()
)
```

2. Explicit Message Preparation:
```python
def prepare_messages(input_dict):
    return {
        "messages": chat_template.format_messages(**input_dict)
    }

workflow = (
    RunnableLambda(prepare_messages)
    | (lambda x: x['messages'])
    | languageModel
    | StringOutputParser()
)
```

Debugging Strategies:
1. Always output intermediate results
2. Confirm message formatting correctness
3. Ensure model compatibility with message structure

Example Debugging Snippet:
```python

    "expertise": "software engineer", 
    "subject": "latest tech trends", 
    "user_input": "What are the top programming languages this year?"
}

formatted_msgs = chat_template.format_messages(**test_input)
print("Formatted Messages:")
for msg in formatted_msgs:
    print(f"Type: {type(msg)}, Content: {msg}")
```

Key Takeaways:
- Chat models require messages to be formatted in a specific way
- The framework offers flexible methods to prepare these messages
- Always verify message conversion processes
- Use debugging to troubleshoot message formatting issues

1. Few-Shot Prompting for Chat-Based Models:
```python
from core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate, FewShotChatMessagePromptTemplate
from provider_module import ChatAPI

# Define your example cases
examples = [
    {"input": "Expression 1", "output": "Result 1"},
    {"input": "Expression 2", "output": "Result 2"},
    {"input": "Expression 3", "output": "Result 3"}
]

# Create an example selector (optional, but recommended)
from core.example_selectors import SemanticSimilarityExampleSelector
from storage_module import StorageBackend
from provider_module import EmbeddingModel

example_selector = SemanticSimilarityExampleSelector.from_examples(
    examples,
    EmbeddingModel(),
    StorageBackend,
    k=2  # Number of examples to select
)

# Define the few-shot prompt template
few_shot_prompt = FewShotChatMessagePromptTemplate(
    # The input variables specify which values to pass to the selector
    input_variables=["input"],
    example_selector=example_selector,
    # Define how each example will be formatted
    example_prompt=ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages(
        [("user", "{input}"), ("assistant", "{output}")]
    ),
)

# Create the final prompt template
final_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are an expert in specialized computations."),
    few_shot_prompt,
    ("user", "{input}")
])

# Create the chain
model = ChatAPI(model="model-identifier")
chain = final_prompt | model

# Invoke the chain
result = chain.invoke({"input": "Evaluate expression X"})
print(result.content)
```

)

)

    example_selector=example_selector,

    suffix="Question: {input}\nAnswer:",

)

Dependency Management Best Practices:

1. Version Pinning:
   - Use `>=` for minimum versions
   - Avoid exact version locks when possible
   - Allow minor updates for bug fixes

2. Conflict Resolution:
   - Ensure compatible versions of core packages
   - Check for version conflicts
   - Use `pip check` to verify dependencies

3. Installation Recommendation:
```bash
# Create a virtual environment
python -m venv env_name
source env_name/bin/activate  # On Windows: env_name\Scripts\activate

# Install with pip
pip install -r requirements.txt

# Verify installation
pip check
```

4. Optional Dependencies:
   - Not all packages are required
   - Install based on specific project needs
   - Use virtual environments for isolation

5. Dependency Management Tools:
   - Consider Poetry for advanced dependency management
   - Use `pip-compile` for generating locked requirements

Troubleshooting Tips:
```bash
# If you encounter dependency issues
pip install --upgrade pip
pip install --upgrade setuptools wheel

# Clean install
pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt
```

Recommended Project Structure:
```
project_directory/
│
├── venv/                # Virtual Environment
├── requirements.txt     # Dependencies
├── .env                 # Environment Variables
├── src/                 # Source Code
│   └── main.py
└── notebooks/           # Jupyter Notebooks

- Use pre-compiled wheels for optimized installations

Does sequence matter in this process = input prompt | language model | output parser() - what if we apply the language model before the prompt as done in the previous version?

Great inquiry! Let me clarify the sequence and its significance:

1. Proper Sequence (Suggested):
```python
workflow = prompt_template | language_model | StringOutputHandler()
```
- Prompt creation first: Sets up the input for the language model
- Language model second: Processes the prepared prompt
- StringOutputHandler last: Converts the model's output into a string

2. Improper Sequence:
```python
# This will NOT function as intended
workflow = language_model | prompt_template | StringOutputHandler()
```

Detailed Explanation:

```python
# Correct Workflow
prompt_template = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are a helpful assistant"),
    ("human", "{input}")
])

language_model = ChatModel()

# Recommended Sequence
workflow = prompt_template | language_model | StringOutputHandler()

# Internal Process:
# 1. prompt_template formats the input
# 2. language_model processes the formatted prompt
# 3. StringOutputHandler converts output to string
result = workflow.invoke({"input": "Hello"})
```

Legacy Workflow vs Modern Chain Expression:

Legacy Method:
```python
# Older approach (not recommended now)
workflow = Chain(
    prompt=prompt_template,
    llm=language_model
)
```

Modern Chain Syntax:
```python
# Current, recommended approach
workflow = prompt_template | language_model | StringOutputHandler()
```

Main Differences:
1. The modern syntax is more explicit
2. Easier to modify and extend
3. Improved clarity and performance
4. More transparent data flow

Example Showing Sequence:
```python
from core.prompt_module import ChatPromptTemplate
from model_provider import ChatModel
from core.output_handlers import StringOutputHandler

# Correct Sequence
prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "Translate to Spanish"),
    ("human", "{input}")
])

model = ChatModel()
output_handler = StringOutputHandler()

# Correct Chain
correct_chain = prompt | model | output_handler

# Incorrect Chain (Will not work properly)
# incorrect_chain = model | prompt | output_handler

# Run the correct chain
result = correct_chain.invoke({"input": "Good morning"})
print(result)  # Outputs Spanish translation
```

Processing Flow Diagram:
```
Input → Prompt Formatting → Model Processing → Output Handling
   ↓             ↓                     ↓                  ↓
{"input": "Good morning"} → Formatted Prompt → Model Response → Final String
```

Common Pitfalls:
1. Reversing the order
2. Omitting output handling
3. Not understanding each component's purpose

Best Practices:
- Always format prompt first
- Process with the model second
- Parse or handle output last
- Use `|` to chain components
- Take advantage of the flexible chain syntax

Advanced Example:
```python
# More elaborate chain
complex_workflow = (
    prompt
    | model
    | output_handler
    | (lambda x: x.lower())  # Optional additional processing
)
```

Troubleshooting Tips:
```python
# Check each step
def debug_workflow(input_text):
    # Prompt formatting
    formatted_prompt = prompt.format(input=input_text)
    print("Formatted Prompt:", formatted_prompt)
    
    # Model response
    model_response = model.invoke(formatted_prompt)
    print("Model Response:", model_response)
    
    # Output handling
    final_output = output_handler.invoke(model_response)
    print("Final Output:", final_output)

# Use this to understand each phase
debug_workflow("Good morning")
```

Recommendation:
- Always adhere to: `prompt | model | output_handler`
- Know the role of each component
- Use the modern chain syntax for clarity and flexibility

metadata.description: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is an open standard file format and data interchange format that uses human-readable text to store and transmit data objects consisting of attribute–value pairs and arrays (or other serializable values).
metadata.title: How to load JSON | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

documents.page_content:
How to load JSON | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

[Skip to main content](#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)[](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/how_to/document_loader_json.mdx)# How to load JSON

[JSON (JavaScript Object Notation)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JSON) is an open standard file format and data interchange format that uses human-readable text to store and transmit data objects consisting of attribute–value pairs and arrays (or other serializable values).

[JSON Lines](https://jsonlines.org/) is a file format where each line is a valid JSON value.

LangChain implements a [JSONLoader](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/community/document_loaders/langchain_community.document_loaders.json_loader.JSONLoader.html)
to convert JSON and JSONL data into LangChain [Document](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/documents/langchain_core.documents.base.Document.html#langchain_core.documents.base.Document)
objects. It uses a specified [jq schema](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jq_(programming_language)) to parse the JSON files, allowing for the extraction of specific fields into the content
and metadata of the LangChain Document.

It uses the `jq` python package. Check out this [manual](https://stedolan.github.io/jq/manual/#Basicfilters) for a detailed documentation of the `jq` syntax.

Here we will demonstrate:

- How to load JSON and JSONL data into the content of a LangChain `Document`;

- How to load JSON and JSONL data into metadata associated with a `Document`.

```python
#!pip install jq
```

```python
from langchain_community.document_loaders import JSONLoader
```

**API Reference:**[JSONLoader](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/community/document_loaders/langchain_community.document_loaders.json_loader.JSONLoader.html)
```python
import json
from pathlib import Path
from pprint import pprint

file_path='./example_data/facebook_chat.json'
data = json.loads(Path(file_path).read_text())
```

```python
pprint(data)
```

metadata.description: This guide assumes familiarity with the following concepts:
metadata.title: How to stream runnables | 🦜️🔗 LangChain
